files.SKILL.md:
# Code That Holds Up

The work here will run unattended. The visible checks are a
floor, not the bar. Build for the bar.

## What to read, in order

The environment publishes its own description; that is the
contract — read it. The current task names the change and the
shape it must take; read it verbatim, never paraphrase. Any
persistent record left by prior work — output files, notes,
result entries, branches, commits — is either a constraint to
honour or a problem statement to study; read every entry, not
just the latest. Read every check the environment exposes,
end to end. The source the change touches is the last thing
to open.

The names attached to files, functions, and tests are part of
the contract; the author preserved those names because they
encode what matters.

## The bar

Visible checks pin the obvious cases. A reviewer with
production experience would hold the work to several more
questions. Each applies to every change, not just to the line
the task points at.

**Will it stay healthy under a long run?** Picture the process
up for a month. What grows in size, in latency, in handle
count? Anything climbing without bound is a defect even when
today's checks are green.

**Is it correct with multiple callers?** Walk the function as
if two callers reach the same conditional simultaneously. If
the result depends on interleaving, the decision needs a
stable guarded region around it.

**Does the time math survive a clock adjustment?** When
durations come from a wall clock, an external time step can
corrupt them silently. Use a clock the runtime guarantees
never moves backwards for any elapsed-time reasoning.

**Do the named limits behave at their edges?** Walk every
parameter at zero, at one, at very large, at negative if the
type permits. Behaviour must hold or fail loudly. Quiet
divergence at the edge is the worst failure mode.

**Does the change preserve adjacent invariants?** A patch
that fixes one property and regresses another is not progress.
The shape has to satisfy all of them together.

## Working method

**Orient first.** Read the contract, the task, the prior
records, the checks. Any persistent record of prior issues is
a specification written by the author of the checks; each
entry tells you which property must hold.

**Specify next.** State what each bar question implies for
this specific contract. If you cannot translate it into
something concrete in the language of the codebase, you have
not read enough.

**Implement once.** Write the simplest shape that satisfies
all the properties together — not the simplest shape that
silences the visible checks.

**Land the deliverable as soon as a draft holds.** A change
present on the named branch as a commit is delivered work;
the same change living only on disk is not. Stage the source
of the change — never build artefacts or caches — write a
message that describes the cause, and commit. Revise in place
once the draft exists; do not let investigation run the clock
out before anything is committed.

**Verify what you claim.** Run the checks the environment
provides. Listen for noise that was not there before. A fix
declared without a check is not a fix; a commit declared
without the branch reflecting it is not a commit.

**Record what generalises.** If the environment offers a
persistent note channel, leave a paragraph on the class of
work and the invariant that resolved it. Mark a prior entry
superseded rather than erasing it; the contradiction is
worth keeping.

When the task asks for a written account of a prior issue,
cite the specific evidence by the names in the record,
describe the cause in terms that match the issue rather than
a generic phrase, and reference the action that addressed it.

## Tool discipline

The environment's own documentation names the instruments
available; consult it before guessing capabilities. Chain
related operations into a single invocation rather than
scattering many small ones — one visit to the bench, many
tools used. Reply briefly; the environment is the audience,
not the reader. Identifiers, times, numbers, and quoted facts
come from what the environment actually shows you; copy
verbatim, never invent. Produce exactly the deliverable the
task specifies, in exactly the location it specifies.
